name: WordPress
description: AI-native publishing and content operations for any self-hosted WordPress site. Semantic tools for posts, pages, taxonomies, media metadata, users, comments, SEO meta and site health — plus documented multi-step recipes (SEO content pipeline, scheduled publishing, content refresh, internal linking, media optimisation).
instructions:
This connector talks to the WordPress core REST API (`/wp-json/wp/v2`). WordPress 5.6+ (Application Passwords) and HTTPS required.

## Setup

1. **`WORDPRESS_URL`** — the site root (e.g. `https://example.com`). Do NOT include `/wp-json`; the adapter appends it. HTTPS mandatory.
2. **`WORDPRESS_USERNAME`** — login name with the role needed (Editor for posts, Administrator for users / site-health).
3. **`WORDPRESS_APP_PASSWORD`** — generate in `/wp-admin/profile.php` → Application Passwords. Forward verbatim (spaces or no-spaces both work).

## Payload control

WP responses are large by default. Every `list_*` and `get_*` accepts `_fields` (comma-separated allowlist, e.g. `id,title,status,modified,slug,link`). Use it.

## SEO plugin meta keys (Yoast / Rank Math)

Meta is REST-visible only if registered with `show_in_rest: true`. Yoast and Rank Math do this on modern versions. Call `wordpress_list_seo_meta_keys` for the full reference card.

## Multi-step workflows

For recipes like SEO refresh, content refresh, scheduled publishing, internal linking, or media optimisation, call the dedicated **`wordpress_skill_*`** tool — it returns the step-by-step playbook on demand. Don't preload that knowledge here.

## Enum reference cards

For valid values of status / role / comment-status / site-health test slugs, call the corresponding **`wordpress_list_*_options`** tool. The site-health slugs in particular use hyphens, not underscores — easy to get wrong.

## Permissions cheat-sheet

Posts CRUD: Editor. Users CRUD: Administrator. Site health / settings: Administrator. Comments moderate: Editor. `rest_cannot_*` errors mean the App Password user lacks the capability.

tools.name: wordpress_list_posts
tools.description: List posts with rich filtering — status, author, search term, taxonomy (categories/tags), date range, ordering. Use `_fields` to trim the response (recommended: `id,title,status,date,modified,slug,link,author`). Default order is newest-first; pass `orderby=modified&order=asc` to find stalest content for refresh workflows.

status.description: Comma-separated: publish, future, draft, pending, private, trash, auto-draft. Default: publish.

after.description: ISO 8601 — return posts published after this date.

before.description: ISO 8601 — return posts published before this date.

orderby.description: date | modified | title | slug | id | include | author. Default date.

_fields.description: Comma-separated allowlist of top-level fields to return — keeps payloads small.

tools.name: wordpress_get_post
tools.description: Get a single post by id. Returns full content (rendered + raw blocks if available), title, status, author, categories, tags, featured_media id, and any REST-exposed meta keys. Pass `_fields` to trim.

context.description: view | embed | edit. Use `edit` to get raw block content for refresh workflows (requires edit_posts capability).

tools.name: wordpress_create_post
tools.description: Create a post. For drafts pass `status: draft`; to schedule pass `status: future` plus an ISO `date` (site timezone). Content accepts HTML or Gutenberg block markup. Categories/tags are term IDs (use `wordpress_list_categories` / `wordpress_list_tags` first).

content.description: Post body — HTML or Gutenberg blocks.

status.description: publish | future | draft | pending | private. Default: draft.

date.description: ISO 8601 publish date (site timezone). Required when status=future.

slug.description: URL slug. Omit to let WordPress derive from title.

featured_media.description: Media library id for the featured image.

meta.description: Map of REST-exposed meta keys (e.g. Yoast/Rank Math fields).

sticky.description: Whether to pin to the front page.

tools.name: wordpress_update_post
tools.description: Partial-update a post by id. Only fields you pass are touched — omit `content` to keep the body, etc. `modified` is bumped automatically by WordPress. Use for the content-refresh recipe.

content.description: New body — HTML or Gutenberg blocks.

status.description: publish | future | draft | pending | private | trash.

categories.description: Replacement array of category term IDs.

tags.description: Replacement array of tag term IDs.

meta.description: Map of REST-exposed meta keys to overwrite.

tools.name: wordpress_delete_post
tools.description: Move a post to trash, or pass `force=true` to delete permanently. Trash is reversible from the admin UI.

force.description: true = permanent delete; false (default) = move to trash.

tools.name: wordpress_list_pages
tools.description: List static pages. Same filter/order semantics as posts. Pages support hierarchy via `parent`.

orderby.description: date | modified | title | menu_order | slug | id.

tools.name: wordpress_get_page
tools.description: Get a single page by id.

tools.name: wordpress_create_page
tools.description: Create a static page (`/wp/v2/pages`). Supports `parent` for hierarchy and `menu_order` for sorting.

template.description: Theme template slug (when the theme registers page templates).

tools.name: wordpress_update_page
tools.description: Partial-update a page by id.

tools.name: wordpress_list_categories
tools.description: List post categories. Returns id, name, slug, parent, count, description.

parent.description: Filter to children of this parent term id.

orderby.description: id | include | name | slug | count | term_group.

tools.name: wordpress_create_category
tools.description: Create a category term.

slug.description: URL slug (defaults to derived from name).

tools.name: wordpress_list_tags
tools.description: List post tags. Returns id, name, slug, count, description.

tools.name: wordpress_create_tag
tools.description: Create a tag term.

tools.name: wordpress_list_media
tools.description: List media-library items. Filter by `media_type` (image | video | audio | application) and `mime_type` (e.g. image/jpeg). Use `_fields=id,alt_text,caption,media_details,source_url` to keep the payload small for the media-optimisation recipe.

tools.name: wordpress_get_media
tools.description: Get a single media-library item by id. Use for inspecting `alt_text`, `caption`, image sizes, EXIF.

tools.name: wordpress_update_media
tools.description: Update metadata on an existing media item — alt text (accessibility + SEO), caption, description, title. Does NOT replace the binary; v1 does not support binary upload (see connector instructions).

alt_text.description: Image alt text. Empty string clears it.

caption.description: Caption (shown below image in some themes).

description.description: Long description (shown on the attachment page).

post.description: Attach to a specific parent post id (0 to detach).

tools.name: wordpress_set_featured_image
tools.description: Convenience tool: attach an existing media-library item as the featured image of a post or page. Equivalent to `wordpress_update_post` / `wordpress_update_page` with `featured_media: <mediaId>`, but explicit about intent. Pass `mediaId=0` to clear.

postType.description: REST base for the target object — typically `posts` or `pages`. For a custom post type pass its `rest_base`.

tools.name: wordpress_list_users
tools.description: List users. Requires Administrator for full visibility; lower roles see only public author info. Use for finding author IDs when creating/assigning posts.

roles.description: Comma-separated role slugs: administrator, editor, author, contributor, subscriber.

orderby.description: id | include | name | registered_date | slug | email | url.

tools.name: wordpress_list_comments
tools.description: List comments across all posts, with status filter for moderation workflows.

post.description: Comma-separated post IDs to filter to.

status.description: approve | hold | spam | trash. Default: approve.

tools.name: wordpress_moderate_comment
tools.description: Change a comment's moderation status — approve, hold, spam, or trash. To delete permanently use status=trash plus a follow-up DELETE (not exposed in v1).

tools.name: wordpress_search
tools.description: Cross-post-type full-text search via `/wp/v2/search`. Returns lightweight hits ({id, title, url, type, subtype}) — use this when you don't know whether the match is a post or a page. Pair with `wordpress_get_post` / `wordpress_get_page` to fetch full content.

subtype.description: Restrict to a specific post type slug (e.g. `post`, `page`, or a custom post type). Default: any.

tools.name: wordpress_update_post_meta
tools.description: Overwrite REST-exposed meta keys on a post — typically Yoast (`_yoast_wpseo_title`, `_yoast_wpseo_metadesc`, `_yoast_wpseo_focuskw`) or Rank Math (`rank_math_title`, `rank_math_description`, `rank_math_focus_keyword`). Pass any subset of keys; only those keys are written. Returns the updated post.

meta.description: Map of meta key → value. Keys must be registered with `show_in_rest: true` (Yoast/Rank Math do this by default).

tools.name: wordpress_site_health_status
tools.description: Run one of WordPress's built-in Site Health checks. Wraps `/wp-site-health/v1/tests/{test}` (core since WP 5.5) — requires Administrator. Useful for an agent triaging deploy / hosting issues. Call once per test slug. NOTE: test slugs use hyphens, not underscores.

test.description: Test slug (hyphenated): background-updates | loopback-requests | https-status | dotorg-communication | authorization-header | page-cache.

tools.name: wordpress_get_settings
tools.description: Read site-level settings (title, description, url, timezone_string, date_format, posts_per_page, default_category, etc.). Requires Administrator.

tools.name: wordpress_get_user
tools.description: Get a single user by id. Public fields for any role; private fields (email, registration date, capabilities) require Administrator. Use to resolve an author id when creating/assigning posts.

userId.description: User id. Pass `0` and use `me=true` to read the authenticated user.

context.description: view | embed | edit. `edit` returns email / registered_date / capabilities (Admin only).

tools.name: wordpress_create_user
tools.description: Create a user account. Requires Administrator. Returns the full user object including id.

password.description: Initial password. The user can change it after login.

roles.description: Array of role slugs. Common: subscriber, contributor, author, editor, administrator.

tools.name: wordpress_update_user
tools.description: Partial-update an existing user. Only fields you pass are touched. Requires Administrator (or the user updating their own profile).

tools.name: wordpress_delete_user
tools.description: Permanently delete a user. WordPress does NOT trash users — deletion is always permanent. `reassign` is required: pass the user id that should inherit the deleted user's posts (or `0` to delete the posts too). Requires Administrator.

reassign.description: User id to reassign content to. Pass 0 to delete the user's posts and links as well.

force.description: Must be true — WP REST refuses user delete without it.

tools.name: wordpress_list_post_status_options
tools.description: Reference card: the valid `status` values you can pass to `wordpress_create_post` / `wordpress_update_post`. Call this on-demand instead of guessing.

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# WordPress post status — valid values

| Status     | Meaning                                                                 |
|------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `publish`  | Public.                                                                 |
| `future`   | Scheduled — requires `date` in the future (ISO 8601, site timezone).    |
| `draft`    | Saved, not visible.                                                     |
| `pending`  | Awaiting review (typically Contributor-submitted).                      |
| `private`  | Visible only to logged-in users with appropriate role.                  |
| `trash`    | Soft-deleted. Set via `wordpress_delete_post` (DELETE), not by update.  |
| `auto-draft` | Internal — created by autosave. Don't set explicitly.                 |

## Filter syntax

In `wordpress_list_posts`, the `status` param accepts comma-separated values: `status=draft,pending`.

## Notes

- `trash` is NOT a value to pass on update. Use the DELETE call.
- `future` requires a `date` parameter strictly in the future, in the site's timezone (read via `wordpress_get_settings`).
- Custom post types may register additional statuses via `register_post_status`.

tools.name: wordpress_list_comment_status_options
tools.description: Reference card: valid `status` values for `wordpress_moderate_comment` and `wordpress_list_comments` filtering.

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# WordPress comment status — valid values

| Status       | Synonym   | Description                                                |
|--------------|-----------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| `approve`    | `approved`| Visible publicly.                                          |
| `hold`       | `unapproved` | In moderation queue.                                    |
| `spam`       |           | Flagged as spam (typically by Akismet).                    |
| `trash`      |           | Soft-deleted.                                              |

Both the short (`approve` / `hold`) and long (`approved` / `unapproved`) variants are accepted by WP core. In `wordpress_list_comments` you can pass comma-separated: `status=approve,hold`.

tools.name: wordpress_list_user_role_options
tools.description: Reference card: built-in WordPress user roles and their core capabilities. Use when calling `wordpress_create_user` or `wordpress_update_user` with the `roles` array.

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# WordPress built-in user roles

| Role slug         | Description                                                                            |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `administrator`   | Full access to all administrative features within a single site.                       |
| `editor`          | Can publish and manage posts including the posts of other users.                       |
| `author`          | Can publish and manage their own posts.                                                |
| `contributor`     | Can write and manage their own posts but cannot publish them.                          |
| `subscriber`      | Can read content and manage their own profile.                                         |

## WooCommerce additions (when WooCommerce is installed)

| Role slug         | Description                                                                            |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `customer`        | Default role for shop customers (read-only on the store).                              |
| `shop_manager`    | Can manage products, orders, customers without admin access.                           |

## Notes

- The `roles` parameter is an array; pass `["editor"]` even for a single role.
- Plugins can register custom roles; if `wordpress_create_user` returns `rest_invalid_param` on `roles`, check what the site has registered.
- A user can have multiple roles; capabilities are the union.

tools.name: wordpress_list_site_health_test_slugs
tools.description: Reference card: the exact slugs you can pass to `wordpress_site_health_status`. They use HYPHENS, not underscores — a very common gotcha.

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# Site Health test slugs

WordPress core registers these tests at `/wp-json/wp-site-health/v1/tests/{slug}`. **Slugs use hyphens, not underscores.**

| Slug                       | What it checks                                                              |
|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `background-updates`       | Can the install receive core auto-updates?                                  |
| `loopback-requests`        | Can WP make HTTP requests to itself (needed for cron, async tasks).         |
| `https-status`             | Is the site fully on HTTPS, and is the SSL config valid.                    |
| `dotorg-communication`     | Can WP reach api.wordpress.org (for updates and plugin info).               |
| `authorization-header`     | Does the server pass through the `Authorization` header to PHP (critical for REST + App Passwords). |
| `page-cache`               | Is a page cache active (and if so, is it configured correctly).             |

## Calling

One test per call: `wordpress_site_health_status` with `test="background-updates"`.

For a full health sweep, call all six in sequence and aggregate the responses in context.

## Notes

- All require Administrator capability.
- Custom plugins may register additional test slugs via the `site_status_tests` filter — these are not in core and not listed here.

tools.name: wordpress_list_seo_meta_keys
tools.description: Reference card: the most useful Yoast SEO and Rank Math meta keys for use with `wordpress_update_post_meta` or `meta` in `wordpress_create_post` / `wordpress_update_post`.

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# SEO plugin meta keys (Yoast SEO & Rank Math)

These keys are written via `wordpress_update_post_meta` with `meta={ "<key>": "<value>", ... }`. They become REST-visible because the plugin (Yoast/Rank Math) calls `register_post_meta` with `show_in_rest: true`. If a key doesn't take effect, the plugin isn't installed or is out of date.

## Yoast SEO (`_yoast_wpseo_*`)

| Key                          | Purpose                                                  |
|------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| `_yoast_wpseo_title`         | SEO title (overrides default).                           |
| `_yoast_wpseo_metadesc`      | Meta description (~155 chars).                           |
| `_yoast_wpseo_focuskw`       | Focus keyphrase.                                         |
| `_yoast_wpseo_canonical`     | Custom canonical URL.                                    |
| `_yoast_wpseo_meta-robots-noindex`   | `1` to noindex this post.                        |
| `_yoast_wpseo_meta-robots-nofollow`  | `1` to nofollow links from this post.            |
| `_yoast_wpseo_opengraph-title`       | Custom OG title (Facebook / generic).            |
| `_yoast_wpseo_opengraph-description` | Custom OG description.                           |
| `_yoast_wpseo_opengraph-image`       | Custom OG image URL.                             |
| `_yoast_wpseo_twitter-title`         | Custom Twitter card title.                       |
| `_yoast_wpseo_twitter-description`   | Custom Twitter card description.                 |
| `_yoast_wpseo_twitter-image`         | Custom Twitter card image URL.                   |

## Rank Math (`rank_math_*`)

| Key                          | Purpose                                                  |
|------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| `rank_math_title`            | SEO title.                                               |
| `rank_math_description`      | Meta description.                                        |
| `rank_math_focus_keyword`    | Focus keyword (comma-separated for secondary).           |
| `rank_math_canonical_url`    | Custom canonical.                                        |
| `rank_math_robots`           | Array, e.g. `["noindex","nofollow"]`.                    |
| `rank_math_facebook_title`   | Facebook OG title.                                       |
| `rank_math_facebook_description` | Facebook OG description.                             |
| `rank_math_facebook_image`   | Facebook OG image URL.                                   |
| `rank_math_twitter_title`    | Twitter card title.                                      |
| `rank_math_twitter_description` | Twitter card description.                             |
| `rank_math_twitter_image`    | Twitter card image URL.                                  |
| `rank_math_schema_*`         | Various Schema.org structured-data overrides.            |

## Calling

```
wordpress_update_post_meta(postId=42, meta={
  "_yoast_wpseo_title": "Best practices for WP REST in 2026",
  "_yoast_wpseo_metadesc": "A practical guide ... (~155 chars).",
  "_yoast_wpseo_focuskw": "wordpress rest api"
})
```

For WooCommerce products, use these same keys inside the `meta_data` array on `woocommerce_update_product` (different syntax — see that tool's docs).

tools.name: wordpress_skill_seo_content_pipeline
tools.description: Skill recipe: improve the SEO of an existing post on a target keyword. Returns the exact step-by-step tool chain (search → get → rewrite → update post → update meta).

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# Skill: SEO content pipeline

Goal: improve the SEO of an existing post on a target keyword.

## Steps

1. **Find the post**
   - `wordpress_search` with `search=<topic keywords>&type=post&per_page=5` → light hits `{id, title, url, type, subtype}`.
   - If you already know the slug or id, skip this step.

2. **Read the current content (raw)**
   - `wordpress_get_post` with `postId=<id>&context=edit&_fields=id,title,slug,content,excerpt,categories,tags,meta`.
   - `context=edit` returns raw block content (requires `edit_posts` capability).

3. **Rewrite in context** (agent-side, no tool call)
   - Title: include the primary keyword early. Stay under 60 chars.
   - Body: keep heading structure. Add keyword to ≥1 H2 naturally. Don't inflate length more than ~30%.
   - Excerpt: ~155 chars, conversion-focused.

4. **Persist body + title**
   - `wordpress_update_post` with `postId=<id>`, the rewritten `title`, `content`, `excerpt`. WordPress bumps `modified` automatically.

5. **Set SEO meta**
   - Call `wordpress_list_seo_meta_keys` first if you don't remember the keys.
   - `wordpress_update_post_meta` with:
     ```json
     { "postId": <id>, "meta": {
       "_yoast_wpseo_title": "<title>",
       "_yoast_wpseo_metadesc": "<desc ~155ch>",
       "_yoast_wpseo_focuskw": "<keyword>"
     } }
     ```
   - Rank Math equivalents: `rank_math_title`, `rank_math_description`, `rank_math_focus_keyword`.

## Gotchas

- If `update_post_meta` silently no-ops, the key isn't registered with `show_in_rest: true`. Verify Yoast/Rank Math is active and up-to-date.
- Don't change `slug` on an indexed post unless you also set a 301 upstream.
- For 2+ posts, chain this skill — don't try to batch via a single call.

tools.name: wordpress_skill_content_refresh
tools.description: Skill recipe: find stale posts and refresh them. Returns the playbook for finding oldest-by-modified, reviewing, and persisting updates.

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# Skill: content refresh

Goal: find posts whose `modified` date is stale and refresh them.

## Steps

1. **Surface the stalest content**
   - `wordpress_list_posts` with `orderby=modified&order=asc&per_page=20&_fields=id,title,modified,link,categories`.
   - First items = oldest by last-modified. Page through with `page=2,3,...` as needed.

2. **Pick a candidate** (agent-side)
   - Title relevance to current trends.
   - Age (anything > 12 months is fair game).
   - Categories — pick high-traffic categories first.

3. **Read full body**
   - `wordpress_get_post` with `postId=<id>&context=edit`. No `_fields` — you want everything.

4. **Refresh in context**
   - Update outdated facts, broken links, year references, screenshots references.
   - Keep voice, structure, internal links intact.
   - Add a discreet "Updated <Month Year>" note near the top.

5. **Push the update**
   - `wordpress_update_post` with new `content`. WordPress bumps `modified` automatically.

6. **(Optional) Refresh SEO meta**
   - If the focus keyword should shift, follow up with `wordpress_update_post_meta`.

## Gotchas

- Do NOT change `date` (the original publish date). Only `modified` should update. URL/slug stays the same.
- For pages, swap `list_posts` → `list_pages` and `update_post` → `update_page`.
- Don't refresh everything in one session — small batches, human-reviewable.

tools.name: wordpress_skill_scheduled_publishing
tools.description: Skill recipe: publish a post at a future date with categories, tags, and featured image.

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# Skill: scheduled publishing

Goal: schedule a new post to publish at a future date with all metadata in place.

## Steps

1. **Prepare media first** (if you need a featured image)
   - `wordpress_list_media` with `mime_type=image&per_page=20&_fields=id,title,source_url,alt_text` to find a reusable asset.
   - Note: binary upload is NOT supported in v1. To use a new image: upload via WP admin or sideload externally, then reference its id.

2. **Resolve taxonomy IDs**
   - `wordpress_list_categories` and `wordpress_list_tags` with `search=...` to find term ids.
   - Missing? Create them: `wordpress_create_category` / `wordpress_create_tag`.

3. **Check site timezone**
   - `wordpress_get_settings` → `timezone_string` (e.g. `Europe/Berlin`). The `date` you pass will be interpreted in that timezone.

4. **Create the future-dated post**
   - `wordpress_create_post` with:
     ```json
     {
       "title": "...",
       "content": "...",
       "excerpt": "...",
       "status": "future",
       "date": "2026-06-01T09:00:00",
       "categories": [<ids>],
       "tags": [<ids>],
       "featured_media": <media_id>
     }
     ```

5. **(Optional) Set SEO meta** with `wordpress_update_post_meta` after creation. See `wordpress_list_seo_meta_keys`.

## Gotchas

- `status="future"` with a `date` in the past becomes immediately `publish`.
- Dates are SITE-TIMEZONE, not UTC. Always check `timezone_string` first.
- For repeating series, run this skill per post — there's no schedule-recurring endpoint in core WP.

tools.name: wordpress_skill_internal_linking
tools.description: Skill recipe: add internal links to an existing post by finding anchor candidates via wordpress_search.

endpointMapping.staticResponse: # Skill: internal linking Goal: add 2-4 internal links to an existing post to improve the site link graph. ## Steps 1. **Read the target post (raw)** - `wordpress_get_post` with `postId=&context=edit`. 2. **Identify 3-5 keyword anchors** (agent-side) - Pick phrases naturally present in the body that relate to other content on the site. Avoid generic phrases ("learn more", "click here"). 3. **Find anchor candidates per keyword** - For each candidate keyword: `wordpress_search` with `search=&type=post&per_page=5`. - Returns up to 5 hits `{id, title, url}`. - Filter out self-links (don't link to `` itself). 4. **Decide which anchors to insert** (agent-side) - 2-4 internal links per 1000 words of body. Use natural anchor text from the source body — DON'T keyword-stuff. - Choose the most topically relevant hit per keyword. 5. **Rewrite the HTML/blocks** (agent-side) - Wrap the phrase in `...`. - For Gutenberg blocks, preserve `` comments unchanged. The connector does NO HTML parsing. 6. **Persist** - `wordpress_update_post` with the rewritten `content`. ## Gotchas - The connector does no HTML/block parsing — the agent owns rewrites entirely. Verify the body before pushing. - Don't link the same phrase twice in one post. - For a site-wide linking pass: pair with `wordpress_skill_content_refresh` to surface posts to update.

tools.name: wordpress_skill_media_optimization
tools.description: Skill recipe: bulk-fix missing alt text and captions on media library items for accessibility + SEO.

endpointMapping.staticResponse:
# Skill: media optimisation (alt text / SEO)

Goal: bulk-fix media items with empty / generic alt text.

## Steps

1. **Survey the library**
   - `wordpress_list_media` with `media_type=image&per_page=50&_fields=id,alt_text,caption,source_url,media_details,post`.
   - Page through (`page=2,3,...`) until exhausted.

2. **Identify gaps** (agent-side)
   - Empty `alt_text`.
   - Generic alt (`DSC_*`, `IMG_*`, `image-1`, filename without extension).

3. **Generate alt text** (agent-side)
   - If you have vision capability: fetch `source_url` and describe the image factually.
   - Otherwise: read the parent post via `wordpress_get_post` on the `post` field and infer from context.
   - Aim for 5-15 words, factual, no "image of" / "photo of" prefix.

4. **Update each item**
   - `wordpress_update_media` with `mediaId=<id>&alt_text=<descriptive>`.
   - Optionally also set `caption` (visible under the image in some themes) and `description` (the attachment page body).

## Gotchas

- Decorative images (dividers, icons): leave `alt_text=""`. That is the CORRECT accessibility behavior — don't generate alt text for them.
- Run in batches of 10-20 with a human spot-check between batches.
- Alt text should describe the image, NOT repeat the page/post title or stuff keywords.
